text: Upon entry and docking, the surgeon begins stripping the peritoneum to ensure the mesh will sit directly against the fascia.
The surgeon dissects the fat associated with the falciform ligament and peri-umbilical region, clearing the area around the defect to create a suitable landing zone for mesh placement.
The surgeon closes a relatively small defect transversely using a permanent V-lock suture.
The surgeon performs a straightforward defect closure using a permanent V-lock suture, running the suture back on itself to secure it in place.
After closing the defect, the surgeon positions the mesh, placing a stitch in its central portion. The mesh is adjusted, revealing the need for additional preperitoneal dissection.

The surgeon ensures the mesh lays flat against the fascia, using absorbable V-lock sutures to fix the mesh by running them from one side to the other. In the background, the needle from the fascial closure is visible, used to tack up the mesh.

The surgeon positions the mesh securely against the abdominal wall using a circumferential baseball stitch to prevent movement. The mesh is carefully aligned to avoid exposure of barbs, ensuring stability during the procedure.
The surgeon secures the intraperitoneal mesh, with some barbs exposed, while ensuring the mesh is properly positioned against the abdominal wall.

The surgeon completes circumferential fixation of a ten-centimeter mesh within the abdominal cavity. For meshes larger than ten centimeters, an absorbable Vicryl suture is typically run down the middle to enhance the mesh-tissue interface.
